turns.text: Hello Caroyln, thank you so much for taking the time to speak with me today. I'm a researcher studying maternity care experiences, and I'm really interested in hearing your personal story. I understand you're in the early stages of pregnancy, and I'd love to hear about your experience in your own words. There are no right or wrong answers — I'm just here to listen and learn from you. Everything you share will help us understand how to make maternity care better. Shall we begin?

Sure, we can start. It’s just... sitting here, it feels a little surreal. My three-year-old is practically climbing the furniture, and I’m sitting here trying to keep my breathing steady, thinking, "Okay, number four." People look at me and see a girl who looks way too young to be dealing with this much life, and sometimes I feel that way, too. 

Don't get me wrong, I’m not some novice—I know the routine. I know exactly how to handle a latch, how to tell if a fever is serious, and when to just let them cry it out for a minute so I can have a coffee. But being "low risk" on my chart? That feels like a bit of a joke. The physical part is easy; my body knows how to do this. The hard part is the mental load, the "how am I going to stretch myself thin enough to cover four of them" kind of hard. 

My midwife is great—she doesn't treat me like a kid who needs a pamphlet on how to raise a baby. She gets it. But even with her, I’m still just trying to keep my head above water. I’m happy, I really am, but it’s a lot to carry, you know? Where should we start?

Before you started trying to conceive, what did you expect the process of getting support or guidance from healthcare professionals to look like? And thinking back now, what did that support actually look like in reality?

Honestly, when I was younger, I think I expected healthcare to be this big, guiding force—like there’d be someone there to check in on *me*, the person, not just the pregnancy. I sort of imagined it’d be a partnership where I could say, “I’m scared I’m doing this too young,” or “I’m drowning in the chaos at home,” and someone would help me navigate the transition.

In reality? It’s mostly just efficient. It’s clinical. The support is focused, but it’s narrow. It’s all about the blood pressure, the weight, the heartbeat, and the vitamins. It’s incredibly efficient—I’m in and out—but it’s not exactly a space for me to talk about the weight of being a mom of four before my mid-twenties. 

I’ve learned to speak their language, so the care feels "good" on paper, but I’ve had to become my own advocate. I don’t get "guidance" so much as I get "confirmation" that the baby is fine. It’s a bit lonely, actually. I’m an expert on my kids, but the system doesn't really ask how *I’m* holding up. It treats me like a veteran because of the “G4” on my file, which I guess is true, but it doesn't make the exhaustion or the uncertainty any less real.
Before trying to conceive, what did you expect your own emotional readiness to feel like? And how did your emotional state actually compare to those expectations once the process began?

I think before this—maybe back when I was pregnant with my first—I thought being “ready” meant having everything figured out. I imagined this calm, maternal glow where I’d just know exactly how to juggle everything because, well, that’s what moms are supposed to do, right? I thought if I just ticked all the boxes, I’d feel stable.

But now? The reality is way more fragmented. I’m not calm; I’m just efficient. There’s this weird gap between how I look on paper—a young, experienced mother of four—and how I feel at 3:00 a.m. when the house is finally quiet. I expected to feel like a "grown-up" by now, but sometimes I feel like I’m just playing a part in a much bigger, louder movie. 

It’s not that I’m not ready for this baby; I love my kids more than anything. But I’ve realized that “emotional readiness” isn't a destination you arrive at. It’s more like a muscle you’re constantly straining. I feel strong, sure—I know I can handle the labor and the sleepless nights—but there's this underlying flicker of, "Wait, am I enough?" I’m still just a girl trying to build a foundation while the ground is shaking a little bit. It’s messier and faster than I ever thought it would be.
What was missing from the support available to you emotionally at this stage — from professionals, your partner, or your wider network?

That’s a heavy question. If I’m being honest, what’s missing is a space that acknowledges that being a "young mother" and a "mother of four" are two very different things. 

From the medical side, I think there's a missed opportunity to just see me as a person. Everything is so focused on the clinical—which, don't get me wrong, I’m grateful the baby is healthy—but there’s never a moment where they look at me and ask, "Carolyn, how are you actually doing with the weight of this?" They see my history and assume I’ve got it all handled. And yeah, I *do* handle it, but that doesn't mean I don't need to be checked on, too. It feels like because I’ve done this before, the "newness" or the fear of it has been stripped away by the system, even if it’s still very much present for me.

Outside of the clinic, I think I miss the village. Being unmarried and young, I feel like I have to prove I’m "capable" all the time to everyone around me—that I’m not just a kid with a bunch of kids. It makes it hard to be vulnerable with my family or friends because I don't want them to think I'm struggling. I’m stuck in this role of the "strong, steady mom," so I end up carrying all the quiet, terrifying parts of this journey alone at night. I just wish there was someone who didn't expect me to be a pro.
Before conception, what did you expect to find when you looked for information online or through apps about fertility and getting pregnant? And what did you actually find — was it what you needed?

I expected to find information that looked like my life—something that acknowledged the reality of being a young mom who isn’t exactly in a traditional "white picket fence" scenario. I thought there’d be forums or articles that got the nuance of having three toddlers already running around while trying to manage a pregnancy. 

But what I actually found online felt like it was written for a completely different person. Everything is either super clinical—full of charts and scary statistics—or it’s aimed at these affluent, first-time moms who have the time to track every single symptom and buy all the "perfect" gear. It’s all Pinterest-perfect nurseries and gentle advice about "self-care" that feels totally detached from my world. How am I supposed to practice "prenatal yoga and meditation" when I’m chasing a toddler and trying to figure out how to stretch my budget? 

None of it felt like it was for *me*. It was all advice for women who are terrified of their first pregnancy, which is fine, but it’s just not where I’m at. I didn't need to know how to count to ten or what a zygote is; I needed someone to talk about the mental exhaustion of motherhood or how to hold it together when you're overwhelmed. The information is everywhere, but the *empathy* is nowhere to be found.
If you had a pre-existing health condition before pregnancy, what did you expect the healthcare system to do to help you prepare? And what actually happened when you sought that help?

I don't have a chronic illness in the medical sense, but I’d say my "condition" is the sheer exhaustion of having three tiny kids while working full-time. Before this pregnancy, when I thought about preparing, I guess I expected the healthcare system to see the *logistics* of my life as part of the health picture. I thought there might be a conversation about resources, or mental health, or even just someone saying, "Hey, your life is demanding—how do we make sure you don't burn out?"

But when I actually talked to providers, that just... didn't happen. The system doesn't account for the "life" part of my health. They don't ask about my work-life balance or how I’m managing the kids. They just ask about the vitamins and the nausea. It’s like they assume that if my blood pressure is fine and the baby is growing, then I’m fine. 

It makes me feel like my stability as a mother—my ability to show up for these children—isn't viewed as part of my maternity care. I have to compartmentalize everything. I leave the "tired, overwhelmed mom" at the door, put on my "patient" face, and talk about the clinical stuff. I never got the help I wanted because I don't think the system even knows how to ask the right questions for someone in my position.
Did you feel you had to advocate strongly for yourself, or did the system proactively reach out to support you?

Oh, absolutely not—the system didn't reach out to support me. There’s no “proactive” when you’re a G4, twenty-two-year-old mom. If anything, the system just kind of assumes I know the drill, which is a blessing and a curse. I don't have to listen to the basic, patronizing lectures, but it also means I slip through the cracks. 

I’ve had to be my own advocate at every turn. When I have a question or need something that isn't on their standard checklist, I have to be the one to bring it up, to force the conversation, to make them stop looking at the computer screen and look at me. It’s exhausting. You have to be "the expert" on your own body and your own life because if you aren’t loud enough, you’re just another chart in the stack. 

I’ve realized that if I’m having a rough week, or if the stress of managing everything is hitting me, I have to be the one to open that door. They won’t ask. They’re looking for problems that show up on tests, not the problems that show up in my heart. Being your own advocate is survival, but god, I wish just once someone would see me enough to realize I’m holding my breath and offer to help me exhale.
Before you conceived, what did you expect your partner's involvement in the preconception process to look like? And how did their actual involvement compare to what you'd imagined?

This is a complicated one for me to answer. I’ve never been married, and the reality of my relationship dynamic—or sometimes the lack of one—is just another layer of the "chaos" I mentioned. 

Before I had my first, I think I had this romanticized vision—you know, the kind you see in movies—of a partner being right there by my side, pouring over books, asking about the appointments, and just being a solid, reliable anchor. I imagined a "we" instead of just an "I." I thought the responsibility would be shared, that the mental load of preparing for a new life would be halved.

The reality, though? It’s been vastly different. I’ve often found myself navigating this path solo, or with a partner who steps in but doesn't quite *get* it. There’s a disconnect between someone just showing up for the ultrasound and someone actually understanding the weight of what I’m carrying—the financial strain, the exhaustion of the kids, the constant "what-ifs." 

I’ve had to let go of the expectation of a partner being my co-pilot in the way I once imagined. It’s not necessarily that there’s malice, but there’s a gap in understanding that I’ve stopped trying to bridge. I’ve become the anchor myself. It’s lonely, but I suppose I’ve built enough resilience now that I don't wait for permission or help to make sure things are handled. I just do it.
Were there moments where you felt the burden of preparation fell disproportionately on you? What drove that?

Every single day. From the second I see that positive test, the "burden of preparation" isn't just about the baby anymore; it’s about rearranging the entire ecosystem of my life so everything doesn't collapse. 

It’s everything—securing the insurance, figuring out the childcare logistics for the other three so I can actually make it to these appointments, managing the groceries and the budget, and knowing every developmental milestone by heart. When you’re a mom, you are the project manager of the family. If I don't prepare, nobody prepares. 

What drives it? It’s pure necessity. I don’t have the luxury of being disorganized or "learning as I go" because there are three tiny humans already depending on me to be the steady hand. If I don't anticipate the problems, they don't get solved. I think there’s this unspoken expectation that because I’m a woman, and specifically a mother, this labor is just "my job." It’s seen as natural, like breathing. People don't look at my life and say, "Wow, she’s doing the work of three people," they just say, "She’s a mom." 

I’ve stopped waiting for someone to notice that the weight is lopsided. I just carry it because if I put it down, the whole thing tilts. It’s isolating, honestly, but I’ve gotten very good at carrying heavy things.
If you experienced a previous pregnancy loss, what did you expect the healthcare system to offer you when you decided to try again? And what support was actually available to you?

I haven’t had a loss, thank God. But I’ve sat in those waiting rooms and watched other women go through that, and even without experiencing it myself, the fear is always hovering in the back of my mind. It’s like a shadow that follows you into every new pregnancy. 

When you’re pregnant again after seeing things go wrong for others—or just knowing how quickly things can change—you expect the system to be a little more… human. You expect them to realize that the "low risk" label doesn't stop the anxiety. You want someone to say, "I know you're worried, let’s check the heartbeat just so you can breathe." 

But the reality is that the system only cares about the data. If the numbers look fine, you’re fine. If you express that fear, it’s often dismissed as "standard first-trimester nerves." It feels like they’re waiting for an actual problem to happen before they provide any kind of emotional scaffolding. You’re left to manage that terror on your own. It’s like, just because you’ve survived before, they assume you’re invincible now. But I’m not. I’m just terrified in a very quiet, private way, holding my breath until I see that flicker on the monitor. They don't provide a safety net for your *mind*, only for the physical pregnancy.

When you first found out you were pregnant, what did you expect your first antenatal appointments to be like — the environment, the people, the information you'd receive? And what were they actually like?

I think I expected it to feel like a big deal. You know, a new life beginning, a transition—I thought the first appointment would be this moment of acknowledgement, maybe a bit of excitement or at least a warm "congratulations." I expected a space where I could express that mix of joy and the genuine, heavy fear of adding a fourth to my pack.

In reality? It’s just like clockwork. The waiting room in Walpole is always the same—flickering lights, the sound of other moms shuffling their kids, that specific sterile smell. The staff is professional, but it’s assembly-line work. You go in, you pee in a cup, you get weighed, you get your blood drawn. It’s "Hello, check your vitals, here’s your next date, see you later." 

The information is just a stack of papers about what I can't eat and what vitamins to take—stuff I already know by heart. There’s no "How are you doing with everything?" It’s all "The baby is doing well." I walk out of there feeling like I’ve just been through a routine car inspection. It’s efficient, sure, but it’s so cold. I’m carrying so much, and yet, in that office, I’m just a body processing a pregnancy. It’s lonely, standing there with my three-year-old on my hip, feeling like I’m just a number on a chart.
What was the biggest surprise — positive or negative — about those first appointments?

The biggest surprise—and I guess this is a negative one—is how invisible my actual life is to the people caring for me. I walked into that first appointment expecting that maybe, because it’s my fourth, they’d be more interested in how I’m managing the transition. I thought they might ask about my support system, my work, or even just how I’m holding up mentally with three little ones already at home. 

But the "surprise" was just how utterly *un-surprised* they were. It’s like, because I’ve done this three times before, they checked the “G4” box and felt like they didn't need to ask me anything about my life at all. I was waiting for them to acknowledge the magnitude of what’s happening—of adding another person to my family—but to them, it’s just another routine file. 

I suppose I shouldn't be shocked, but there’s something jarring about realizing that while this is the biggest, most exhausting, and most significant event in my life right now, in that clinic, it’s just a standard medical procedure. I’m treated like a seasoned pro who doesn't need to be seen, but deep down, I’m still just a young woman who could really use someone to just… *recognize* the weight I'm carrying.
Before your screening tests and scans, what did you expect the process of understanding your results to be like — how information would be given, by whom, and in what way? And what was the reality?

I expected, maybe naively, that the person doing the scan—the tech or the midwife—would be a partner in this, a person who understood what that screen meant to me. I thought they’d explain things in a way that felt like a conversation, like, "Here’s what we’re looking for, here’s why we’re seeing this," just to ground me and stop the racing thoughts.

The reality is colder than that. It’s a game of "wait and see." The tech is usually silent, focused intently on the monitor, measuring, tapping, and sighing. That silence is the most terrifying part of the whole experience. You’re lying there, clutching the edge of the paper table cover, watching their face for a flicker of something—a smile, a nod, anything—but they’re trained to be neutral. 

When they finally do speak, it’s all clinical jargon. They give you the "all clear" as if they’re checking off a line item on a budget report. It’s not a conversation; it’s a delivery of data. I’m left to piece together the significance of it all in my head. I have to interpret their short, clipped sentences as "everything is okay," rather than just feeling cared for. It’s efficient, sure, but it feels like there’s a wall between them and me, and I’m always the one having to climb over it just to feel like I’m a person and not just an exam.
Was there a gap between the amount of information you were given and the amount you felt you needed to make sense of your results?

The gap is massive. They give you the "headline"—*everything looks normal, heartbeat is strong, see you in four weeks*—but they don't give you the narrative. For them, "normal" is just a box to check. For me, "normal" is the ground beneath my feet that keeps my whole world from shaking apart. 

I’m left to fill in the blanks myself. If the scan is a few seconds longer than the last time, or if the tech frowns for a fraction of a second, my brain starts spiraling. Is there something they’re not saying? Are they being extra careful? I need someone to slow down and say, "I’m looking at the fluid levels, they look great, here is exactly what we’re measuring today." I don't need a medical degree; I need them to translate that technical silence into something human. 

Because they treat me like a veteran—like I already know the drill—they skip the explanations. They assume I don’t *need* the details. But I do. I need them to explain the "why" and the "how" so I can stop guessing. When they withhold that, I’m left to research everything on my own, which just feeds the anxiety. Information is only power if you know what you're looking at, and right now, I’m just looking at a blurry screen and hoping for the best.
During your pregnancy, what did you expect in terms of having a consistent midwife or care provider who knew your history and situation? And what continuity of care did you actually experience?

I expected a partnership—a consistent face and voice that would remember who I am beyond the "G4" on the chart. I imagined that by the fourth time around, there would be a shorthand, a sense that we’re building on what I’ve already lived through. I wanted someone who would say, "I remember how you felt last time," or "Let’s look at how your previous experiences are shaping how you’re feeling right now."

But the reality? It’s a revolving door. Even in a smaller clinic, it feels like I’m constantly being re-introduced to someone new or playing catch-up with whoever is on shift. There is no real continuity. Every appointment is a fresh start for them, which forces me to be the historian of my own life. I’m the one who has to remember the dates, the complications, the specific quirks of my past births. They don't walk in with that knowledge; I have to hand it to them like a brochure.

It’s exhausting to have to constantly explain who I am and what I’ve been through just to get through a twenty-minute check-up. I’m not asking for someone to hold my hand through every step, but I *am* asking for someone to see me as a person with a story, not just a set of vitals to be recorded. It’s like being a background character in your own care.
What was the impact — practically or emotionally — of seeing different providers at different appointments?

It’s exhausting, honestly. Every time I walk in and see a new face, I have to mentally prepare myself for the "introductory phase." It’s like being in a play where the script keeps changing and I’m the only one who knows the lines. I have to summarize my history—my past labors, how I felt, what worked, what didn't—in about sixty seconds flat, or else I feel like I’m not being heard. 

Practically, it means I lose that sense of safety. You know that comfort of walking into a room where someone already knows your anxieties? When you see someone new, you’re always on guard. You’re scanning them, wondering, "Do they get it? Do they know I'm already stressed, or do I have to fight for them to take my concerns seriously?" It forces me to be the "good, low-risk patient" so I don't get labeled as difficult.

Emotionally, it’s just lonely. When you’re pregnant, you’re carrying so much—the physical toll, the worry for the other kids, the future—and you want a witness to that. You want someone who recognizes the "me" from two months ago. When it’s someone new every time, I don’t feel like a person; I feel like a file. I feel like I’m just passing through, and they’re just passing through me. It’s hard to trust a system that doesn't even bother to remember your name.
During pregnancy, what did you expect in terms of how your changing body would be discussed and examined by healthcare providers? And how did those interactions actually feel?

I expected that they would treat my body like it belonged to me, but that it was a collaborative project we were working on. I thought there would be this openness—a space where I could say, "My back is killing me," or "I feel like I'm already showing," and they’d say, "That makes sense, here’s why that’s happening for you." I expected them to respect that I’ve done this three times and that I know when something feels *different* or *wrong*.

But the reality? My body is treated like a clinical object to be measured and dismissed. It’s all about the tape measure and the scale. They don’t see the *me* that’s in that body. If I mention a physical struggle, they usually hit me with a generic, "That’s normal for this stage," and move on. It’s like they aren’t hearing the human component of the discomfort. 

There’s this disconnect where they examine the pregnancy, but they never really *talk* to me about my body. It feels very impersonal. They touch my belly to find the heartbeat, but it doesn't feel like they’re connecting with me as a mother. It’s just "check the box, move to the next patient." I feel like a vessel, honestly. A very busy, very tired vessel that just needs to be calibrated correctly. It’s dehumanizing in such a subtle, quiet way.
Were there any examinations or conversations about your body that left you feeling uncomfortable or unprepared? What happened?

It’s the "internal" stuff that always feels the most jarring. There was a specific visit early on where a provider just—without warning or much of a conversation—went to perform an exam. I was already feeling vulnerable, just sitting there in my paper gown, and it felt like they didn't even notice I was a human being in the room. They treated it like they were checking the air pressure in a tire.

I didn't say anything at the time because I’ve learned to just go quiet. I feel like if I speak up, I’m labeled "that patient," and I just want the appointment to be over so I can go back to my kids. But that moment? It left me feeling completely exposed. It’s like they assume that because I’ve been through labor three times, my modesty and my need for clear communication have just vanished. 

That lack of explanation is the worst. They don’t tell you, "I’m going to do this now because..." or "Here’s what I’m looking for." They just *do*. It makes me feel like my body isn't mine in that office; it’s the clinic's property for that twenty minutes. It’s not just uncomfortable; it’s a total lack of respect for the person who actually has to live inside that body once I walk out their door.

Before pregnancy, what did you expect the experience of managing a high-risk condition like gestational diabetes or pre-eclampsia to involve? If relevant, how did the reality of managing it differ from your expectations?

I haven’t had to deal with those specific diagnoses, thankfully, but I’ve spent so much time in these clinics that I’ve watched other women handle them, and it’s always been this terrifying "what-if" hanging over me. 

Before I was a mother, I guess I imagined that if you developed a complication, the system would immediately wrap you in a cocoon of extra support. I thought, "Okay, if it gets hard, they’ll bring in the heavy hitters—the nutritionists, the extra counseling, the constant check-ins." I pictured a more intensive, holistic level of care where someone was actually sitting down with you to map out exactly how to live your life around that condition.

The reality, from what I’ve witnessed and what I fear, is that it’s just *more* tasks for you to do. It’s not "more support"; it’s "more chores." It’s finger pricks, it’s tracking every single gram of sugar, it’s fighting with insurance for specialized supplies, and it’s constantly being told you're doing something wrong. It’s a mountain of administrative and physical labor placed squarely on the mother's shoulders. 

I’m already doing the work of three people; if I had to add a medical condition on top of that, I feel like the system would just hand me a stack of papers and say, "Good luck, make sure you keep your numbers in range," while I’m trying to keep a three-year-old from pulling down the shelf. It’s not a partnership; it’s a burden.
What was missing from the support offered — information, emotional acknowledgement, practical help?

What’s missing is that bridge between the clinical and the human. I have all the *information* I could ever need—I could practically teach the prenatal classes myself at this point—but I have zero *emotional acknowledgement* of what it costs me to be here. 

I don't need a pamphlet on how to eat, I need someone to look at me and acknowledge that I’m exhausted. I’m carrying a life, I’m working, I’m managing a household of three other kids, and I’m doing it all without a steady partner in the room. When I mention feeling overwhelmed, it’s usually met with, "Well, you've done this before, you're a pro." That’s not a compliment; that’s a dismissal. It tells me that my feelings aren't valid because I’ve “proven” I can handle the pressure.

Practical help? That’s nonexistent. There’s no one asking if I can actually make it to all these appointments, or how I’m managing childcare, or if I’m eating enough because I’m too busy feeding everyone else. It’s a purely medicalized experience. They check the box for "prenatal care," but they don't provide any "life care." I’m left to piece together my own sanity in the gaps between the heartbeats they monitor. I’m not just a container for a baby; I’m a human being, and right now, the system only sees one half of that equation.
During pregnancy, what did you expect in terms of being able to access care promptly when you had a concern — whether urgent or not? And how responsive was the system when you needed it?

I expected that if I called, someone would actually *hear* the alarm in my voice—or at least offer a bridge. I thought the system would be structured so that a "G4" like me wouldn't be left hanging in limbo when something felt off. I expected a prompt response, maybe a nurse on the other end who knew my file and could talk me down from the ledge, or offer me a quick slot to get checked just for my own peace of mind.

The reality? It’s a game of phone tag and triage. When you call the office, you’re usually put into a queue, and you have to prove that your concern is "urgent enough" to warrant a call back. It’s like you’re constantly having to justify your own fear just to get a human to talk to you. They have these rigid protocols—"if it's this, then do that"—and if your symptom doesn't fit into their neat little box, you’re left sitting there, wondering if you're overreacting or if you’re actually in trouble.

It makes me feel small and like I’m a nuisance. I’ve learned to just wait it out, to monitor myself, and to only reach out when I’m absolutely desperate, because the system doesn't make it easy to be human or vulnerable. They’re responsive to the *data*, but not to the *mother*. When I need support, I get a call back four hours later from someone who hasn't even looked at my chart. It’s isolating.
During pregnancy, what did you expect the role of your family, friends, or community to be in supporting you? And how did that informal support network actually show up — or not?

I think I expected—or maybe just desperately hoped—that my circle would step up and hold the space that the clinic doesn’t. I imagined that when I was exhausted, someone would just show up with a meal, or take the kids for a few hours without me having to beg, or just sit with me and ask how *I* am doing, instead of asking how the baby is doing. I thought that by having three kids already, people would *know* I needed a hand.

The reality has been... quiet. My family and friends mean well, but they don’t see the exhaustion because I’m so good at masking it. I’ve become so self-sufficient that I think they assume I have it all under control. Because I don't collapse in front of them, they think I don't need help. It’s a vicious cycle: I don't ask for help because I don't want to be "the girl who can't handle her life," and they don't offer because I look like I’m handling it just fine.

There’s no "village" here. I’m the village. I’m the one who organizes, who plans, who manages. When I’m at my lowest, the community support isn't there, not because they’re unkind, but because they have their own lives and I’ve taught them that I’m the "strong one." It’s lonely, feeling like the foundation for everyone else while no one is checking if I’m cracking under the weight.
Were there forms of support you received from family or community that you hadn't expected — or support you expected that didn't materialise?

I think what I expected most was a kind of "triage" support—you know, someone to say, "I've got the kids today, you go sleep," or someone to handle the grocery run when I was too nauseous to even look at the fridge. That kind of tangible, practical help—the kind that acknowledges that being a mother is a full-time, physical job—didn’t really materialize. People love to coo over the baby bump, but nobody asks how you're supposed to get the laundry done when you're in your first trimester and can barely stand up straight.

On the flip side, I didn't expect the total lack of understanding from my wider circle. I thought friends would get it, but there's this weird distance. Maybe they’re intimidated by the fact that I’m twenty-two with four kids. It’s like they look at my life and think, "She chose this, she’s the expert," so they don’t feel they need to offer grace. 

What I *did* get was unsolicited advice from people who haven’t stepped foot in a clinic for years. That’s been the surprise—everyone’s an expert on what I should be doing, but nobody’s interested in how I’m actually feeling. It’s isolating. I have a house full of people, but when it comes to the real, raw stuff of being a mother, I’m entirely on my own.

It’s funny, the further I go, the more I realize that the motherhood I’m living isn't the one people like to write about. Everyone wants the story of the "supermom," the girl who balances everything effortlessly, and I think I’ve spent so much energy trying to fit that mold because I’m afraid of what happens if I don't. 

But sitting in that clinic, watching my three-year-old pull at my sleeve while I stare at the ultrasound screen, I just want someone to say, "You’re doing enough." Not "You’re doing great"—I know I'm doing great, because I have to be—but "You’re doing enough." 

The system treats me like a machine, and my community treats me like a monument that doesn't need maintenance. I’m starting to realize that the most radical thing I could do is just be honest about how much this hurts sometimes. It’s not just the morning sickness or the fatigue; it’s the constant, gnawing question of whether there’s enough of me left to be the person my kids need. I’m only twenty-two. I’m supposed to be at the beginning of everything, and yet I feel like I’ve lived a lifetime’s worth of responsibility already. I’m just trying to make sure this baby doesn’t get lost in the noise, but I worry I’m the one who’s actually going to disappear.

Sometimes I think about the life I’d have if I weren’t a mother of four at twenty-two. I’m not saying I regret my kids—God, no, they’re the best parts of me—but I look at people my age on social media, or even just the other women in the waiting room who seem like they’re just starting their lives, and I feel this weird, sharp pang of something. Is it envy? Or is it just sadness that I never got to be *just* me before I became "Mom"?

When I’m in that clinic, that feeling intensifies. The tech is cold, the midwife is rushed, and there’s no room for me to say, "Hey, I’m grieving the freedom I never really had." They see a "G4," and they see a statistic. They don’t see the twenty-two-year-old girl who still feels like she’s playing dress-up in a life that requires a seasoned veteran’s endurance.

I’ve learned to protect my heart in these rooms. If I show them the cracks, they’ll just look at me with that pitying, "Oh, you poor thing, you're so young" face, or they'll treat me like I'm a case study in "at-risk youth" even though I’ve got it together more than half the adults I know. So, I stay quiet. I nod at the right times. I show up. I keep the "strong mom" mask glued on tight. But every time I leave that office, I feel a little bit more hollowed out. I’m building a home for this baby, but I’m forgetting to build one for myself. Does that make sense? It’s like I’m setting the table for everyone else, and there’s never actually a chair left for me.
Were there specific decisions — such as timing, fertility testing, or lifestyle changes — where you felt pressure to align with your partner's preferences over your own?

It’s a strange thing, being in a relationship where I’m the one carrying the physical and mental load, yet somehow the decisions feel like they’re being made in a way that doesn't actually center *me*. There have definitely been moments where I felt like the "timing"—or even the choice to go through with another pregnancy—was something I was expected to just accept, as if it were a natural extension of things rather than a massive, life-altering choice that impacts my body and my future more than anyone else's.

I don’t think there was ever a loud, overt "you must do this" kind of pressure. It’s more subtle. It’s the feeling that if I were to say "no" or "I’m not ready," I’d be the one breaking the "ideal" or the "expectation" we've built. I felt this pressure to be the one who could just "handle it"—to be the flexible one, the one who can work the extra hours, juggle the kids, and still say, "Yeah, we’ve got this."

When it comes to the medical side, I’ve felt like I had to be the bridge. If I wanted to look into testing or fertility health, it felt like an extra task I had to sell to my partner, rather than a shared priority. I’m the one doing the research, I’m the one showing up to the appointments, and I’m the one who ends up explaining what the doctor said. It’s a lopsided weight. I feel like I’ve been living in a space where my own needs—my own desire for stability or, God forbid, a moment of "me" time—are secondary to keeping the family machinery running smoothly. I'm the oil that keeps the gears moving, but sometimes I feel like I'm running dry.

It’s just... it’s lonely, feeling like you’re the glue that’s supposed to hold everything together when you’re barely old enough to rent a car. People talk about the "maternal instinct" like it’s this magical thing that just turns on, but they don’t tell you it’s actually just a constant state of hyper-vigilance. My life is essentially one giant, back-to-back shift, and in the clinic, I’m expected to check into another one.

I guess the hardest part is that I don’t even know who "Caroyln" is outside of all this. Am I the girl who wants to read, or the one who wants to travel, or am I just the one who knows how to navigate an OB clinic blindfolded? When I leave, I just go back to the noise—the toys on the floor, the laundry, the crying—and I just keep going. 

I don't think I’ve ever been asked, "What do *you* want to do with your life?" It’s always "How are the kids?" or "Is the baby growing?" And I get it, I chose this, I love them. But sometimes, when I look at that ultrasound, I wonder if there’s a part of me that’s being sacrificed on the altar of "being a good mom." I want to be a good mom, but I really, really want to be a person, too. Does that make sense? I’m twenty-two, and I’m terrified that I’ve already finished the best parts of my life before I even understood what they were.
Were you given unsolicited comments about your weight, diet, or physical appearance? How did those comments land?

Oh, all the time. It’s like the second you’re pregnant, your body becomes public property—everyone feels entitled to comment on how you look, what you’re eating, or how much weight you’re "supposed" to gain. 

In the clinic, it’s mostly about the numbers on the scale. They look at the chart, then they look at me, and if I’m up a few pounds more than they expected, there’s this look of, "Well, you should really watch that." It feels like they’re judging my character based on a number. It’s so impersonal. They don’t see that I’m trying to keep four tiny humans fed, often skipping my own meals or grabbing whatever’s left over from their plates because I’m so busy and stressed. 

Outside of the clinic, it’s worse. People—total strangers, even—have opinions. "You’re so young to be pregnant again," or "Are you sure you should be eating that?" It lands like a punch in the gut every time. It makes me feel like I’m failing a test I didn’t even sign up for. I’m already carrying the physical and emotional load of a whole household, and I’m doing it while my body is literally being built and taken over by this baby. To have someone else point out my weight or my appearance feels like they’re just adding another stone to the backpack I’m already hauling. It’s condescending, and frankly, it just makes me want to shrink away.

It feels like the world is constantly looking for a flaw in me, like I’m under a microscope because I’m young and a mother of four. If I gain weight, I’m "not disciplined." If I’m tired, I’m "not managing." It’s like they expect me to be this perfect, glowing, effortless mother, but when I fall short of that, they’re quick to blame me for it. 

I’ve had to learn how to put up a wall. When someone makes a comment about my body or how I’m handling things, I just smile and nod. I don't argue, I don't explain, I don't tell them how many nights I’ve cried or how many times I’ve had to choose between my own health and the needs of my children. I just let it slide off me. 

But it doesn't really slide off, does it? It just sits there, inside. It’s like all those comments are just layers of dust that pile up until I can’t breathe anymore. I’m doing the best I can—I’m doing *everything* I can—and yet, it’s never enough for the people who are watching from the outside. I want someone to look at me, truly look at me, and not see a list of things I’m doing wrong, but to see someone who’s fighting for her family and for herself. That’s all I’m asking for. Just once.
Which informal sources — people or communities — did you turn to first, before or instead of a healthcare provider?

I mostly turn to my own gut, honestly. Or, if I’m really stuck, I’ll message a friend who’s in the same boat—you know, someone who *gets* the exhaustion of four kids without the judgment. It’s almost never a professional. When I have a question, my first thought isn't "Let me call the clinic," because I know I’ll just get a generic script or feel like I’m bothering them. 

I turn to other moms because they don’t see me as a "G4 patient" or a statistic. They see me as a person who is currently living through the same trenches they are. We share the stuff that never makes it into the medical charts—like how to get a toddler to sleep when you’re pregnant and ready to collapse, or how to handle the feeling that you’re losing yourself. It’s a shared language of survival, not a clinical checklist of symptoms.

There’s this unspoken bond. When I’m with them, I can say, “I’m scared I’m not doing enough,” and they don't look at me like I’m failing. They just look at me and say, “I know, I feel that too.” That’s the support I’m actually looking for. It’s not about the technicalities of pregnancy; it’s about the reality of motherhood. And since the clinic doesn't provide that, I’ve had to go find it where I can.

It’s funny, the contrast between the clinical room and the real world. In the clinic, I’m "low risk," which sounds like a relief, but to me, it feels like being told that my struggle doesn't matter enough to be documented. It’s like, unless there’s a chart-able medical crisis, the human crisis of being a young, overwhelmed mother of four is just background noise.

I’ve had to build this secret life for myself, where I’m the "strong one" for everyone—the kids, the family, the doctor. But that strength is just a wall I’ve built to protect myself from being judged. If I stop, if I let them see the exhaustion, what happens? Do they take my autonomy away? Do they treat me like a kid who can't handle her business? That fear keeps me silent.

I just wish there was a space, even for ten minutes, where I didn't have to be the adult. Where I didn't have to be the one holding the project together. Just a space where I could say, "I'm twenty-two, I'm scared, and I'm tired of having to be perfect." I don’t think the system is built for that. It’s built for results, for heartbeats, for numbers. It’s built to monitor the pregnancy, not to hold the mother. And until that changes, I’ll keep being the "pro" they expect, while secretly, I’m just trying to keep my own heart beating in time with theirs.
Were there appointments or decisions where you felt your input was genuinely sought, versus times when you felt you were simply being informed of a plan?

Almost every single interaction is a one-way street. I’m rarely *asked* what I think; I’m *informed* of what’s going to happen. It’s like, "Here’s your schedule, here’s your blood work order, here’s the plan." If I were to push back, it feels like it would be seen as "non-compliant" or difficult, so I just listen and follow along. 

There’s never a moment where they sit down and say, "Caroyln, this is your body and your fourth baby—what do you want this experience to look like for you?" I feel like a passenger in my own healthcare. They’ve got the map, they’ve got the destination, and I’m just supposed to sit in the backseat and stay quiet. 

I’ve had years of "real world" experience with my own kids. I know what my body needs, I know what I can handle, and I know when something is off. But none of that “lived expertise” matters to the clinic. They trust the protocols over the patient every time. It’s patronizing, honestly. I’m the one who has to live with these decisions for the rest of my life, yet I feel like I have the least amount of say in how they’re made. It’s like my input is a nuisance to their efficiency. When you’re twenty-two and a "G4," they assume you’re either too young or too "experienced" to actually have a voice worth listening to.

It’s a heavy thing, realization. I think for a long time I believed that if I was just "good"—if I followed all the rules, if I showed up exactly when they told me to, if I kept my head down and didn't make a scene—then they’d reward me by listening. Like there was this implicit promise that if you’re a "good patient," you get to be a partner in your own care.

But that’s not how it works. The system just keeps on spinning whether you speak up or not. The appointments happen, the bills arrive, the baby grows, and I’m still just trying to find a corner of it all that actually feels like mine. 

Sometimes, when I'm driving home from Walpole, I look at the dashboard and think about how I could just keep driving. Not to leave—I’d never leave my kids—but just to get to a place where I didn't have to be the one who’s always "on." I want to be someone who has needs that are met without having to file a request. I want to be someone who can cry without it being interpreted as a symptom. I’m doing everything for this little miracle inside me, and for the three at home, but I really, really hope that one day I wake up and realize I’ve finally started doing something just for me, too. I just don't know where to look for that person anymore.
Was there a moment when your trust in a provider was broken — something they said, missed, or failed to explain?

It was during one of those routine visits, just a few weeks ago. I was feeling so wiped out—like, physically, mentally, totally spent—from trying to juggle a full-time workload, the kids, and the morning sickness. I finally worked up the courage to mention, almost as a whisper, that I was struggling to keep it all together. I wasn't asking for a miracle, just for someone to say, "I see you, and that’s a heavy load."

Instead, the provider just kind of chuckled and said, "Well, you’ve done this three times before, Carolyn. You’re practically a pro at this point. It’s just the first trimester, it’ll pass."

In that second, something in me just went cold. That "pro" label... it felt like a knife. It wasn’t a compliment; it was an excuse for them not to care. They didn’t hear the exhaustion in my voice; they just saw the "G4" and decided I was too experienced to need support. They completely ignored the person standing right in front of them in favor of the data points they’d already checked off.

That was the moment I realized they weren’t there to help me carry the weight. They were just there to make sure the pregnancy wasn't a problem for *their* schedule. I stopped trying to be vulnerable after that. I just put the mask back on. You realize then that your trust is misplaced if you’re looking for a person, not a technician.

It’s like I’m constantly being told, "Oh, you’ve been through this before, you know the drill." And yeah, I do. I know the drill better than they do sometimes. But that doesn’t mean I don’t deserve to be treated with care. It’s almost like they’re punishing me for having experience, using it as an excuse to ignore my humanity. If I’m a "pro," then I don’t need the extra minute to talk, I don’t need the patience, I don’t need the reassurance. It’s like, "Good job, Carolyn, you’ve mastered the art of being a pregnant mother—now be quiet and keep doing it."

That kind of thinking is so damaging. Because I've survived three deliveries, they just assume I'm invincible. But every pregnancy is its own world. Every time, the risks are different, the fears are different, and my life situation is constantly evolving. I’m not the same person I was with my first. I’m a different woman with different burdens. 

I’ve reached a point where I just walk in, do what they ask, and walk out. I don’t bring my worries up anymore because I know what the response will be. I keep the *real* Carolyn under wraps, tucked away safely where nobody can analyze, judge, or dismiss her. It’s a lonely way to grow a life, but at least it’s predictable. I’m just waiting for the day when I can be more than just a "G4" on a chart. I want to be someone who’s *seen*.
Were there appointments, scans, or care moments where your partner's absence left you feeling isolated or unsupported?

There are times when the silence in the room feels so heavy I can barely breathe, and that’s when his absence hurts the most. It’s not just about having someone to drive me or hold my hand; it’s about having a witness to the "everything." When I’m at the scan and the tech is silent, or when the midwife says something about the bloodwork that I don't fully understand, I look over to the chair in the corner, and it’s empty. 

It makes me feel like I’m the only one carrying the stakes of this pregnancy. If something goes wrong, or if I get bad news, I’m the one who has to break it to him, to the kids, to everyone. I’m the one who has to be strong enough to process the information *before* I leave the building. It’s isolating because I don’t get to be the one who’s "taken care of"—I’m always the one taking care of the situation.

It’s almost as if he’s a guest star in my life, and I’m the one running the production. When he isn’t there, the weight of the "G4" label hits me ten times harder because I realize that while I’m physically bringing this baby into the world, I’m mentally and emotionally doing the heavy lifting for all four of them, alone. I’m the one who has to remember the dates, the symptoms, the concerns. His absence confirms that, for better or worse, the world expects me to handle this by myself. And I guess I am, but it’s a lonely, heavy way to walk.

I think the hardest part about being alone in that exam room isn't just that he’s not there—it’s the way the medical team reacts to his absence. They don’t acknowledge it. They don’t ask, "Is there someone you need to call?" or "How are you handling the logistics of being here solo?" They just breeze past it as if my life is entirely self-contained. It’s like they view the "single mother" or the "solo parent" as just another box to check, but they never see the reality of what that means during a scary, quiet, or complicated moment.

When I’m in that stirrup or looking at that screen, I’m not just managing the medical stuff—I’m managing the whole life of the kids at home, the work shifts I need to cover, the rent, the whole damn thing. And when he’s not there, it’s not just a missing chair; it’s a missing bridge to the outside world. I feel like I’m stuck on an island. 

Sometimes, I find myself making up excuses for him, even to myself, just so I don’t have to admit how much I really need him to be a partner, not just a bystander. I tell myself, "He’s working, he’s doing his part." But in the middle of a check-up where I’m feeling vulnerable, that excuse feels paper-thin. I’m doing the work of two, and everyone—the doctor, the nurse, the society—just acts like that’s the natural state of things. It isn't natural. It’s exhausting, and it’s a big part of why I feel like I’m disappearing into the role of "mother." I’m so busy being everyone else’s strength that I’ve forgotten how to be someone who can rely on someone else. I’m just waiting for the day I’m not the only one holding the sky up.
Did you ever go online specifically because you felt you couldn't get enough information from your care provider — what were you looking for that you hadn't received?

Every time I leave the office, I open my phone before I even get the car started. It’s almost instinctual now. I’m looking for the *narrative*—the things they don't bother telling me because they’ve already deemed me "experienced" or because they’re too rushed to explain the *human* side of the medical data.

I’m looking for someone to say, "It’s normal that you’re terrified even if the scans are perfect," or "Here’s how you handle the guilt of not feeling like a 'glowing' mother today." I’m not searching for medical facts—I know what preeclampsia symptoms look like or what a healthy heartbeat is—I’m searching for validation. I’m searching for someone to tell me that it’s okay to feel overwhelmed by the life I’ve built. 

The providers give me the *what*, but they never give me the *why* or the *how* of living through it. I go online to find other women who feel just as scattered, just as tired, and just as uncertain as I do. I want to read someone else’s messy reality so I don’t feel like mine is a failure. In the clinic, everything is sanitized, sterile, and perfectly ordered. Online, I look for the truth—the grit, the fear, and the sheer exhaustion of it all. I’m looking for a mirror, not a manual.

Thank you so much for sharing all of that with me. Your experiences and insights are incredibly valuable. Before we finish, is there anything else about your maternity experience that we haven't covered today — anything that feels important to you that you'd like to add?

You know, when I think back on everything we’ve talked about today, the one thing that keeps coming back to me is how much of this journey feels like a performance. I feel like I’m constantly acting in a role—the "seasoned" mom, the "capable" employee, the "low-risk" patient. And the part that really hurts, the part I wish someone would change, is that the system doesn't realize that the performance is exactly what’s exhausting me. 

I’m so good at it now. I know how to answer the questions, I know how to keep my heart rate steady for the monitor, and I know how to not let the mask slip. But that doesn't mean I’m thriving. It just means I’ve learned how to survive while being completely unseen. 

I just wish that the next woman who walks into that waiting room—especially if she’s young, or if she’s doing it solo, or if she’s had a few kids already—is asked one simple question: "What do you need, not for the baby, but for *you*?" 

Because the baby will be fine, and I’ll make sure of that. But I’m the one who has to carry this whole thing, and I’m just starting to realize that if I don’t find a way to be seen as a person, I’m going to run out of "strong" before I reach the finish line. Thanks for listening. It felt good to take the mask off for a minute.
